content: Healthcare quality measurement follows Donabedian's framework: (1) Structure - characteristics of care setting (staffing ratios, board certification rates, technology availability); (2) Process - what providers do (medication reconciliation, timely antibiotics, screening rates); (3) Outcomes - patient health results (mortality, complications, functional status, patient-reported outcomes). Process measures are easier to attribute to provider actions but may not correlate with outcomes. Outcome measures require risk adjustment for case mix but better reflect patient experience. CMS Hospital Compare reports >100 measures across mortality, complications, readmissions, patient experience, and timely/effective care domains.

Quality improvement strategies include: (1) Public reporting - Hospital Compare, Leapfrog ratings creating reputational incentives; (2) Pay-for-performance - Medicare's Hospital Value-Based Purchasing adjusts 2% of payments based on quality scores; (3) Quality improvement collaboratives - hospitals sharing best practices through AHRQ networks; (4) Clinical pathways/standardization - reducing unwarranted variation (e.g., sepsis bundles, ERAS protocols for surgery); (5) Patient engagement - involving patients in safety (ask about hand hygiene, medication lists). Hospitals in top quality quartile have 15-20% lower mortality, 10-15% fewer complications, but quality-payment correlations remain modest (r=0.2-0.3).

Quality measure validity requires: (1) Evidence link between measure and outcomes - HEDIS measures backed by systematic reviews; (2) Risk adjustment adequacy - APR-DRG, Elixhauser comorbidity indices explain 60-70% of mortality variation; (3) Measurement reliability - sufficient sample sizes for stable estimates (≥30 cases/year minimum); (4) Attribution accuracy - assigning outcomes to responsible providers (complicated by care fragmentation); (5) Gaming detection - monitoring for upcoding, cherry-picking patients, or neglecting unmeasured aspects. Composite measures (e.g., CMS Star Ratings) weight components but may mask specific weaknesses. Patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) increasingly used but response bias and case-mix differences challenge comparisons.
